# One-step solvothermal synthesis of Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite as magnetically recyclable mimetic peroxidase



# Zhonghua Wang * , Ming Chen, Jinxia Shu, Yang Li


Chemical Synthesis and Pollution Control Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, China West Normal University, Nanchong637002, PR China


# a r t i c l e i n f o


Article history: Received21February2016Received in revised form10April2016Accepted26April2016Available online28April2016


# Keywords:


One-step method Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite Mimetic peroxidase H2O2detection Magnetic recyclability and reusability


# 1. Introduction


Natural enzymes, due to their high efficiency and mild reaction conditions, have been extensively studied and widely used in thefield of medicine, chemical industry, food processing and agriculture [1,2], environmental monitoring and protection [3,4]. However, natural enzymes have their own intrinsic drawbacks and disadvantages, such as degradation by protease, denaturation under strong acidic and alkaline conditions, and at high temperature conditions, as well as the requirement of specials storage conditions [5,6]. Moreover, the preparation, purification and storage of natural enzymes are usually expensive and time-consuming [7,8]. Therefore, the synthesis of artificial enzyme mimics has long been a major concern of chemists and biochemists [9e13].

Recently, Gao et al. has reported that Fe3O4nanoparticles possess intrinsic enzyme activity similar to that of natural peroxidases such as horseradish peroxidase (HRP), which opens the door to the development of nanoscaled inorganic materials in biochemicalfield [14]. Since this report, a number of nanoscaled metal oxides, metals and metal alloys, such as Co3O4-nanorods [15],

# a b s t r a c t


Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite was prepared by a simple and facile one-step solvothermal method and characterized by XRD, SEM, TEM, EDS and XPS techniques. The Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite showed peroxidase-like activity, it could catalyze the oxidation of typical peroxidase substrates, o -phenylenediamine (OPDA) and3,3 0 ,5,5 0 -tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) in the presence of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). A simple colorimetric method for the detection of H2O2could be established based on the intrinsic peroxidase-like activity of the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite. In addition, the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite displayed good separation ability, stability and reusability, which could be easily separated from the reaction system by an external magnet and reused for the next round of reaction without significant decrease of its catalytic activity. We expect that the as-prepared Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite may have the potential to replace natural peroxidase in thefields of catalysis, green chemistry, biosensors, environmental monitoring and protection.

VO2nanobelts [16], Ag2O nanoparticles [6], Cu nanoclusters [17], Au nanoparticles [18], Au@Pt nanostructures [19] and Au@PdPt alloy nanorods [20], have been investigated and found to possess intrinsic peroxidase-like activity.

Noble metal nanoparticles, such as Pt, Au and Ag nanoparticles, have attracted intensive research interest because of their unique electronic structures and excellent catalytic properties [21e23]. However, the use of noble metals is limited to some degree due to their high cost and limited supply. Compared with noble metals such as Au and Ag, Cu is a much cheap metal [24]. In the past years, much attention has been paid to prepare nanoscaled Cu, Cu2O, CuO and Cu@Cu2O composites and to investigate their application in thefield of catalysis [25,26] and environmental protection [24,25,27]. For example, Liu and co-workers fabricated porous CuO micro-/ nanostructures by a two-step route and probed their peroxidaselike activity [28]. Zhou and co-workers developed a two-step solvothermal method to prepareflower-like Cu@Cu2O nanocomposites and found that some of the Cu@Cu2O nanocomposites exhibited higher photocatalytic performance than pure Cu2O [29]. Ai and co-workers developed an interfacial hydrothermal method for the preparation of Cu@Cu2O core-shell composite microspheres and investigated their visible-light-driven photocatalytic activity [30]. Kou and co-workers synthesized porous Cu@Cu2O nanoparticles through a two-step strategy involving dealloying and

zhwangs@163.com, zhwangs@cwnu.edu.cn (Z. Wang).

surface oxidation, and investigated the photocatalytic performance and adsorption behavior of the as-prepared nanoparticles [24,25]. In order to overcome the problem of recycling Cu@Cu2O nanoparticles from the reaction system, Wang and co-workers introduced magnetic Fe3O4into the Cu@Cu2O nanoparticles and prepared magnetically recyclable Fe3O4@Cu2O@Cu nanocomposites by a two-step method [31]. However, some of the materials are synthesized through complicated and multi-step procedures, and some need toxic organic solvent, or the synthesized materials are difficult to be recycled. Therefore, it is still necessary for material researchers tofind a simple and convenient method to prepare Cu@Cu2O composite with easily recyclable properties. Furthermore, to the best of our knowledge, the peroxidase-like activity of Cu@Cu2O composite has not yet been systematically studied, although there are some publications about the peroxidase-like activity of Cu and CuO [17,28,32,33].

In this study, we reported a simple and facile one-step solvothermal method for the preparation of Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite, and investigated the peroxidase-like activity of the nanocomposite with3,3 0 ,5,5 0 -tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) and o -phenylenediamine (OPDA) as model peroxidase substrates. It was demonstrated that the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite exhibited intrinsic peroxidase-like activity, as it could catalyze the oxidation of TMB and OPDA to produce colored products in the presence of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). Additionally, the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite can be easily magnetically recycled and reused without significant loss of the catalytic activity, indicating that the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite has the potential for the replacement of natural peroxidases in practical applications.


# 2. Experimental



# 2.1. Materials


Copper sulfate pentahydrate (CuSO4 $ 5H2O), iron chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3 $ 6H2O), sodium acetate trihydrate (NaAc $ 3H2O), ethylene glycol (EG), o -phenylenediamine (OPDA), 3,3 0 ,5,5 0 -tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30wt%) were purchased from Chendu Kelong Chemical Reagent Company (Chengdu, China). All reagents were of analytical reagent grade and used as received. Ultrapure water was utilized for the preparation of all aqueous solutions.


# 2.2. Preparation of Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite


The Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite was prepared by a facile one-step solvothermal method. Typically, 1mmol CuSO4 $ 5H2O (0.25g), 2mmol FeCl3 $ 6H3O (0.54g) and20mmol NaAc $ 3H2O (2.7g) were dissolved in25mL EG solution by magnetic stirring. Then the resulting solution was transferred into a50mL Teflonlined stainless autoclave and heated at200 /C14C for10h. After cooled down to room temperature naturally, the product was collected by an external magneticfield, washed with water and ethanol three times, and dried at60 /C14C.


# 2.3. Characterization


Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was taken on an FEI Inspect F50microscope. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) were carried out on an FEI Tecnai G2F20fi eld-emission high-resolution transmission electron microscope. Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) was performed by an energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer (Oxford instrument X-Max). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) was collected by a Rigaku Dmax/Ultima IV diffractometer with monochromatized Cu k a radiation ( l ¼ 1.5418 Å). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on an XPS spectrometer (Kratos XSAM800); the binding energies were adjusted with respect to the C1s binding energy of284.8eV.


# 2.4. Peroxidase-like activity


The peroxidase-like activity of the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite was examined by the catalytic oxidation of OPDA and TMB in the presence of H2O2. Typically, the reaction of OPDA (or TMB) with H2O2was performed at25 /C14C in100mM phosphate buffer (pH8.0) or100mM acetate buffer (pH4.0) in the presence of0.05mg mL /C0 1Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite. Unless otherwise stated, the concentrations of OPDA and H2O2were0.5and2.0mM respectively for the oxidation of OPDA, and the concentrations of TMB and H2O2were0.2and10mM respectively for the catalytic oxidation of TMB.


# 2.5. Kinetics measurements


Kinetics measurements were performed at25 /C14C with0.05mg mL /C0 1Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite in100mM phosphate buffer (pH8.0) by varying the concentration of OPDA (0.2e1.5mM) at afixed concentration of H2O2 (2.0mM) or vice versa by varying the concentration of H2O2 (0.2e6.0mM) at afixed concentration of OPDA (1.0mM). When TMB was used as chromogenic substrate, experiments were carried out in100mM acetate buffer (pH4.0) by varying the concentration of TMB (0.025e0.3mM) at afixed concentration of H2O2 (20mM) or by varying the concentration of H2O2 (2e100mM) at afixed concentration of TMB (0.2mM). The steady-state reaction rates of OPDA and TMB oxidations were obtained by monitoring the absorbance increase at416and652nm using molar absorption coefficients of16.7 [34] and39mM /C0 1cm /C0 1 [35], respectively. The apparent kinetic parameters were determined by nonlinear least squarefitting of the absorbance data to Michaelis-Menten equation ( v ¼ Vmax $ [S]/( Km þ [S])) or Lineweaver-Burk double reciprocal model (1/ v ¼ ( Km/Vmax) $ (1/[S]) þ 1/Vmax), where v is the reaction rate, Vmax is the maximum rate, [S] is the substrate concentration and Km is the Michaelis constant which represents the affinity of enzyme toward substrate, the value of Km is equal to the substrate concentration at which the reaction rate is half of the maximum reaction rate.


# 3. Results and discussion



# 3.1. Characterization of Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite


Fig. 1shows the XRD pattern of the as-prepared Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O sample. The diffraction peaks at2q values of43.30, 50.43, 74.13 /C14can be ascribed to the reflections of (111), (200) and (220) planes of cubic Cu (marked with & ) with lattice constant a ¼ 3.615nm (JCPDS No. 65-9743). The peaks at2q values of30.10, 35.46, 56.98, 62.57 /C14can be ascribed to the reflections of (220), (311), (511), (440) planes of cubic Fe3O4 (marked with ( ) with lattice constant a ¼ 8.391nm (JCPDS No. 65-3107). Additionally, a weak peak at36.50 /C14 (marked with A ) can be indexed to the (111) plane of Cu2O (JCPDS No. 65-3288). The relatively lower intensity for the diffraction peaks of Cu2O may be due to the tiny amounts of Cu2O. Therefore, Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O composite sample was successfully prepared by a simple one-step solvothermal method.

The SEM images show that the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O composite are composed of uniform spherical-like morphology (Fig. 2A and B). The mean diameter of the spherical-like particles is about50nm as revealed by the TEM images (Fig. 2C and D). Fig. 2E shows a typical

Fig. 1. XRD pattern of the as-prepared sample.

which was in agreement with the (111) plane distance of Fe3O4 (0.484nm tabulated from XRD) and the (110) plane distance of cubic Cu2O (0.301nm tabulated from XRD), respectively, while the zone (II) was the superposition area of Fe3O4and Cu2O.

Elemental analysis of the as-prepared Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite was performed with an energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). The results show that the Cu, Fe and O elements coexist in the nanocomposite (Fig. S1A, Supporting information). The EDS elemental mapping results further confirm that the Cu, Fe elements are uniformly distributed in the nanocomposite (Fig. S1B and C, Supporting information).

The elemental composition and chemical status of the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O composite were further analyzed by means of Xray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). XPS results indicated that the composite contained Fe, Cu, O, and C (Fig. 3A). The carbon peak was due to the adventitious hydrocarbon from the XPS instrument itself. The Fe, Cu and part of O peaks were from the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O sample. Fig. 3B shows the XPS spectrum of Fe2p of the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O sample, the peaks at binding energies (BE) of

Fig. 2. The SEM (A and B), TEM (C and D) and HRTEM (E and F) images of the as-prepared Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite.

HRTEM image of the composite and the enlarged view of area F in Fig. 2E is depicted in Fig. 2F. The fringe spacings of crystallites (I) and (III) in Fig. 2F were about0.485and0.293nm, respectively,

712.1and724.8eV were characteristics of Fe3 þ , while the peaks at BE of709.8and722.8eV were characteristics of Fe2 þ . The satellite signal at717.6eV is attributed to the combination of Fe2 þ and Fe3 þ ,

Fig. 3. XPS spectra of the as-prepared Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O sample: (A) survey, (B) Fe2p, (C) O1s and (D) Cu2p.

for the satellite signals of Fe2 þ and Fe3 þ are at715.5and719.0eV, respectively [36,37]. The presence of Fe2 þ and Fe3 þ in the sample further confirms the formation of Fe3O4.

The O1s core-level spectrum is shown in Fig. 3C. The peak at530.1eV is in agreement with the lattice oxygen in Cu2O [38e40] and Fe3O4 [41], the peak at531.5eV can be assigned to the presence of hydroxyl species as a result of the surface-adsorbed water on the air-exposed surface of the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O composite according to previous studies [38,42e44].

The XPS spectrum of Cu2p core level region for the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite displays main peaks at933.3and953.1eV which are attributed to the binding energy of Cu2p3/2and Cu2p1/2, respectively (Fig. 3D). The broad peaks of Cu2p3/2and Cu2p1/2can be further divided into two different peaks at932.8, 934.9eV and952.6, 955.1eV, respectively. The peaks at932.8and952.6eV can be attributed to Cu/Cu2O (Cu0 /Cu þ ) [45], whereas the peaks at934.9and955.1eV can be assigned to CuO/Cu(OH)2 (Cu2 þ ) [44,46]. Cu2 þ can also be distinguished by the appearance of the shake-up satellite features at ~940.6e943.5eV [47] and ~961.9eV (denoted by arrows), which evidenced the open3d9shell of Cu(II) [46,48]. The binding energy at934.9for Cu(II) was higher than the previously reported values for CuO in the range of933.4e933.9eV [40,48]. It has been reported that when the content of CuO is low, the binding energies of Cu2 þ will shift to higher values [49]. Thus the binding energy of CuO at934.9eV in the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O samples may be due to the small content of Cu2 þ on the sample surfaces [50]. From these results it is inferred that the surface of Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O sample is oxidized after being exposed to air conditions. In fact, it has been reported that tiny amounts of Cu2O on the surface could be oxidized to CuO and/or Cu(OH)2species when the surface is exposed in air with humidity [30], and the tiny surface CuO/Cu(OH)2can be detected by XPS, but not by XRD. Similar phenomenon has also been observed in our previous work that Cu2 þ signal can be detected by XPS on the surface of Cu2O, but CuO and Cu(OH)2are not detected by XRD [27]. Based on the differences between the XRD and XPS results, it can be reasoned that XPS is much more sensitive than XRD for surface detection. It has also been reported that XPS only detects signals within the upper5nm thickness on the surface, while the XRD can detect signals in micro-order thickness [30,51].


# 3.2. Peroxidase activity of Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite


The peroxidase-like activity of the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite was examined by the catalytic oxidation of OPDA and TMBinthe presence of H2O2. OPDA and TMB have been widely used as typical chromogenic substrates for investigating the oxidaseand peroxidase-like activities of natural enzymes or mimetic enzymes [6,34,52]. As shown in the insets of Fig. 4A and B, the solutions of OPDA and TMB could react with H2O2to present yellow and blue color, respectively, within60min in the presence of Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite, whereas, the solutions of OPDA and TMB were still colorless in the absence of the nanocomposite.

The absorption spectrum of OPDA oxidized by H2O2in the presence of Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite displayed a characteristic absorption band at ~416nm in the visible region (Fig. 4A) due to the formation of2,3-diaminophenazine (Fig. 4C) [34], while the TMB oxidation product showed two characteristic bands at ~370and ~652nm in the UV e Vis spectrum (Fig. 4B) due to the formation of diamine and diimine as shown in Fig. 4D [52].

Fig. 4. The UV e Vis absorption spectra of (A) OPDA and (B) TMB oxidation products in the presence and absence of Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite (Insets: The photographs of OPDA and TMB solutions oxidized by H2O2in the presence and absence of Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite). The chemical processes of (C) OPDA and (D) TMB oxidation.

However, the solutions of OPDA and TMB showed no obvious absorption in their UV e Vis spectra in the absence of Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite (Fig. 4A and B). These results suggested that the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite possesses intrinsic peroxidaselike activity and can catalyze the oxidation of peroxidase substrates OPDA and TMB in the presence of H2O2.


# 3.3. Identification of active component


Previously, it has been reported that Fe3O4nanoparticles have peroxidase-like activity, which can catalyze the oxidation of peroxidase substrates such as OPDA and TMB in the presence of H2O2 [14]. In order to identify the active component in the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite, we prepared Fe3O4and Cu@Cu2O samples (see Supporting information and Fig. S2) and measured their peroxidase-like activity. As shown in Fig. 5and Fig. S3, the activity of Cu@Cu2O, without the presence of Fe3O4, was a little bit

Fig. 5. Comparison of the peroxidase-like activity of Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite with Fe3O4and Cu@Cu2O for the oxidation of OPDA.

lower than that of Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O. However, without the presence of Cu@Cu2O, the peroxidase-like activity of Fe3O4was dramatically decreased. In addition, the activity of Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O was also a little bit higher than that of the sum of Fe3O4and Cu@Cu2O. These results suggest that the peroxidase-like activity of Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O mainly come from Cu@Cu2O and synergistic effect may exist between Fe3O4and Cu@Cu2O composite.

It was worth of pointing out that the peroxidase-like activity of the Fe3O4nanoparticles reported here was much lower than that of previous report [14]. By carefully comparing the experiment conditions, we found that the size of Fe3O4nanoparticles in our experiment is about50nm, while ~30nm in the literature, and the H2O2concentration used in our experiment was2mM, which was much lower than that used in the literature (530mM). Therefore, the lower peroxidase-like activity of the Fe3O4nanoparticles reported here should be due to the larger particle size and the lower H2O2concentration used in our experiment.


# 3.4. Effects of pH, catalyst concentration, temperature and time of incubation


As we know, the catalytic activity of natural enzymes, such as HPR, is dependent on solution pH, catalyst concentration and temperature, etc. So we examined these factors on the peroxidaselike activity of the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite. The effect of pH wasfirstly examined with the catalytic oxidation of OPDA in100mM phosphate buffer or acetate buffer. The solution pH was adjusted by addition of a small amount of HCl or NaOH solution. For the oxidation of OPDA, the reaction activity increased with increase in solution pH from4.0to8.0, and then decreased with further increase the solution pH up to10.0 (Fig. 6A). As of TMB oxidation, the highest reaction activity was observed at pH4.0 (Fig. S4). This was similar to the effects of pH on the peroxidase activity of HRP and other inorganic materials [53]. The effect of catalyst concentration was studied at thefixed concentrations of OPDA (0.5mM) and H2O2 (2mM). When Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O was varied in the range of0.02e0.05mg mL /C0 1 , the reaction rate was almost proportional to the catalyst dose, but the rate was not significantly increased when

Fig. 6. The effects of solution pH (A), catalyst dose (B), reaction of temperature (C) and time of incubation (D) on the peroxidase-like activity of Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite. Experiments were carried out at standard conditions as stated in the experimental section of2.4, except for the parameter investigated.

further increase in catalyst concentration up to0.075mg mL /C0 1 (Fig. 6B).

The effect of temperature on the peroxidase-like activity of Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite was investigated by varying temperature in the range of25e60 /C14C. As shown in Fig. 6C, the activity of Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O increased with temperature below45 /C14C, while decreased with further increase in temperature. It was worth pointing out that when the oxidation reaction was carried out at60 /C14C, the color of the oxidation product began to fade after several minutes, indicating that the colored product was easily to be degraded by H2O2in the presence of Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite. This might be one of the reasons that the catalytic activity was decreased at high temperature, especially higher than50 /C14C. Similar phenomena are also observed by other researchers when Fe3O4nanoparticles [14] and CuS nanoparticles [54] were used as mimetic peroxidases.

The storage of natural enzymes (proteins), especially enzyme solutions, usually requires low temperature (Usually /C0 80to4 /C14C), for natural enzymes is apt to be deteriorated at high temperature, even at room temperature conditions. To test the temperature stability of the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite, we measured the peroxidase-like activity of the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite after incubation in water at40 /C14C. More than87% catalytic activity of the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite could be retained after30h of incubation (Fig. 6D). Furthermore, the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite could still retain more than90% of its initial peroxidaselike activity within a storage period of three weeks in water at room temperature, further indicating the good temperature stability of the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite as mimetic peroxidase.


# 3.5. Steady-state kinetics study


Steady-state kinetics assays were carried out to further understand the mechanism of the peroxidase-like activity of Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite. The rates of product formation of OPDA and TMB oxidations were determined from the increases in the absorbances at416 [34] and652nm [35], respectively. The experiments were performed withfixed concentration of H2O2and varying the concentration of OPDA (or TMB) or vice versa in the presence of Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite. As shown in Fig. 7and Fig. S5, the plots of reaction rate vs. OPDA (or TMB) and H2O2concentration show typical Michaelis-Menten behavior [55]. The kinetic parameters, such as the Michaelis constant ( Km) and maximum velocity ( Vmax) were obtained by using the nonlinear least squarefitting of the data to Michaelis-Menten equation and also by using the Lineweaver-Burk plots of the double reciprocal model. Data from both models were in good agreement and were summarized in Table1and Table S1. By comparing with the kinetic parameters of other reported inorganic materials and natural peroxidase HRP (Table1and Table S1), it can be seen that the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite possesses intrinsic peroxidaselike activity and shows good affinity toward OPDA, TMB and H2O2. For example, The Km and Vmax values were0.85mM and13.1 /C2 10 /C0 8Ms /C0 1for OPDA and2.3mM and11.9 /C2 10 /C0 8Ms /C0 1for H2O2, respectively. These values are comparable to those of natural peroxidase HRP (Table1).

In addition, the change of the maximum absorbance of OPDA oxidation at416nm was linear increase with the concentration of H2O2from0.4to1.5mM with limit of detection of0.2mM (Fig. S6A). When TMB was used as chromogenic substrate, the absorbance change at652nm was linear increase with the

# 3.6. Recyclability and reusability


To test the recyclability and reusability of the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite, the catalytic oxidation of OPDA in the presence of H2O2was examined. After thefirst round of experiment, the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O catalyst was separated by an external magneticfield from the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O-OPDA-H2O2reaction system and washed with water three times, then the recycled Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O was reused to catalyze the oxidation of OPDA under the same experimental conditions as thefirst round. As shown in Fig. 8A, the

Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite could be successfully recycled by a magnet. The catalytic activity was not significantly lost with three cycles (Fig. 8B).


# 4. Conclusion


In summary, we demonstrated a facile and simple one-step method for the preparation of magnetically recyclable Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite. The as-prepared Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite exhibited highly efficient peroxidase-like activity, it can catalyze the oxidation of peroxidase substrates such as OPDA and TMB in the presence of H2O2. A simple colorimetric method for H2O2detection could be established based on the peroxidase-like activity of the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite. Furthermore, the as-prepared Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite can be easily recycled and reused without significant loss of its catalytic activity. The

Fig. 8. (A) The schematic illustration of the magnetic recycling procedure of Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite. (B) The cyclability of Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite for the catalytic oxidation of OPDA.

recyclability, reusability and nonpolluting nature of the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite would have potential applications in thefield of catalysis, biosensor, environmental monitoring and protection.

# Preparation of Fe3O4and Cu@Cu2O


Fe3O4was prepared by dissolving2mmol FeCl3·6H3O (0.54g) and20mmol NaAc·3H2O (2.7g) into25mL ethylene glycol (EG) solution under magnetic stirring. Then the resulting solution was transferred into a50mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and heated at200 °C for10h. After cooled to room temperature naturally, the product was collected, washed with water and ethanol three times, and dried at60 °C.

Cu@Cu2O was prepared by the same procedure as mentioned above except1mmol CuSO4·5H2O (0.25g) was added to the EG solution instead of FeCl3·6H3O.

Herein, we systematically investigate the influence of crystal phases ( g -Fe2O3and a -Fe2O3) of mesoporous iron oxide (IO) on their peroxidase mimetic activity. In addition, we have also demonstrated the applicability of these mesoporous IOs as nanozymes for detecting the glucose biomarker with a limit of detection (LOD) of0.9m M. See DOI: 10.1039/c9tb00989b (a) determination of H2O2, 5 (b) glucose detection in biological (blood and urine) samples, 6 (c) evaluation of antioxidants7and (d) nanozyme-based colourimetric and electrochemical immunosensors3and nucleic acid sensors. 8Apart from high nanozyme activity, easy separation of NPs or NPs-attached target biomolecules (target biomolecules can be fastened by functionalizing NPs with an antibody or nucleic acid probe relevant to the target) from complex biological systems using an external magnet and re-using them is a highly desirable feature for a biosensor. 9In the past decade, a plethora of NPs and composites have shown enzyme-mimicking properties, and they have been utilized in biosensors for the detection of specific biomolecules. 10Among them, iron oxide (IO) nanostructures, owing to their superparamagnetism, high catalytic activity, low toxicity and ease of synthesis, have been widely utilized as nanozymes. However, many previously reported iron oxides demonstrated their activity at a relatively high temperature (32-45 1C), which limited their applications in biosensors since biomolecules tend to degrade or be denatured at elevated temperatures. 11Recently, several nanozymes have been reported to exhibit mimetic activity at room temperature; however, they required cumbersome modifications with biomolecules ( e.g. , ATP, AMP) or polymer-based engineering ( e.g. molecular imprinting). 12-16Therefore, the preparation of nanozymes with high catalytic activity at room temperature (without any functionalization or modification) is highly desirable. Mesoporous iron oxide with high porosity and surface area, narrow pore size distribution, and controllable wall composition may potentially achieve enhanced peroxidase mimetic activity at room temperature. The high electrocatalytic and enzymatic performance of mesoporous iron oxide in terms of turnover rate and selectivity are contributed by its high active surface area, exposed Fe atoms and enhanced electronic effect. 3,4Moreover, the highly interconnected pores of mesoporous iron oxide could lead to higher fluxes of diffusing redox species over the entire area. Iron(III) oxide (Fe2O3) possesses four crystal phases: alpha- ( a -), beta- ( b -), gamma- ( g -), and epsilon-Fe2O3 ( e -Fe2O3). 17Among all these phases, a -Fe2O3 (hematite) and g -Fe2O3 (maghemite) are the most common and widely investigated phases of iron(III) oxide. 15The stability and unique magnetism of a -Fe2O3and g -Fe2O3render them popular as nanozymes in biosensing platforms. Nevertheless, their intrinsic properties and functionalities largely depend on their morphology, size, and crystal structure. 18,19The a -Fe2O3phase possesses a rhombohedrally-centred hexagonal structure with a close-packed oxygen lattice, 20,21while g -Fe2O3has a cubic crystal structure of an inverse spinel-type lattice structure. 22,23Owing to their different crystal structures, they typically exhibit different activity toward the peroxidase substrate. To date, most of the reported iron oxide nanozymes have either been a -Fe2O3or g -Fe2O3or their composite. Although admirable research and results in the field of IO-based nanozymes have been achieved, few reports have investigated the relationship between their nanozyme activity and crystal phases. To the best of our knowledge, a systematic comparison of the catalytic activity between a - and g -Fe2O3nanozymes towards chromogenic substances has been rarely reported. The detailed mechanism underlying the structural effect of the crystal phases (mesoporous g -Fe2O3and a -Fe2O3) toward the nanozyme activity will be useful for designing artificial nanozymes for future biosensing applications. In this work, mesoporous IOs with two crystal phases ( g -Fe2O3and a -Fe2O3) have been synthesized by a hard-templating approach using mesoporous carbon (CMK-3). The intrinsic nanozyme (peroxidase-mimetic) activity of these two phases toward the oxidation of TMB (in the presence of H2O2) was tested at room temperature and used to determine the crystal phase which could give superior activity. In order to achieve room temperature nanozyme activity, a porous structure is integrated into the surface of both crystal phases. Finally, the as-prepared mesoporous IO samples were employed for both naked eye and colourimetric detection of glucose. Our mesoporous g -Fe2O3sample shows ultrahigh sensitivity toward the detection of glucose with an impressive limit of detection (LOD) of0.9m M, indicating its promising potential for nanozyme-based sensing of glucose and other biomolecules. # 2. Experimental # 2.1Synthesis of CMK-3 Mesoporous silica (SBA-15) was synthesized according to the procedure reported in our earlier work with some modifications. 24-26SBA-15was synthesized to prepare the CMK-3template. The synthesis gel with composition TEOS/P123/HCl/H2O = 1.0/0.017/ 5.9/193.0was hydrothermally treated at413K for24h to form large pores. The obtained products were filtered, dried at373K overnight and calcined at773K for6h. CMK-3was synthesized according to the procedure reported in our previous reports. 25,27In a typical process, 1.0g of dried SBA-15powder was placed into an acidic sucrose solution (1.25g sucrose, 0.14g H2SO4conc. solution, 5.0g H2O). The obtained mixture was then air dried at100 1C and160 1C for a period of6h each. This process was repeated once again to form a coloured sample, which was then carbonized at900 1C under an N2atmosphere for7h. The obtained silica/carbon composite was then etched with a10-15% HF solution to remove the silica template. The etching solution was washed, filtered, and dried at120 1C to yield the CMK-3. # 2.2Synthesis of mesoporous a -Fe2O3and c -Fe2O3 To prepare the mesoporous g -Fe2O3, 26 0.3g of dried CMK-3powder was dissolved in20mL of0.5M iron nitrate solution (in ethanol) and then stirred for6h. The mixture (solution) was then filtered and dried. The dried powder was further treated with0.05M NaOH solution (10mL) and mixed under magnetic stirring for3h, followed by filtration and drying at373K. The dried powder was subsequently calcined at523K in a quasi-sealed container. To remove the carbon scaffold, the obtained sample was heated at573K for2h under a nitrogen atmosphere and at623K for1h under an air atmosphere to achieve the mesoporous g -Fe2O3. The as-obtained mesoporous g -Fe2O3was further heated at623K in the air to obtain the mesoporous a -Fe2O3. # 2.3Nanozyme activity The nanozyme activities (peroxidase-like) of both g -Fe2O3and a -Fe2O3were investigated by using5m g of NPs to catalyze a TMB substrate solution at room temperature. The substrate solution was prepared by dissolving800m M TMB (TMB in dimethylsulfoxide - DMSO) and500mM H2O2in80m L of0.2M sodium acetate (NaAc) buffer solution (pH3.5). The reaction was conducted in the dark for10min, and the nanozyme catalyzed the oxidation of the colourless TMB solution to a blue-coloured solution. The intensity (absorbance at652nm) of the coloured TMB (blue) solution was measured by using a plate reader (Infinite200PRO, Tecan Life Science) in time scan mode. To obtain the steady-state kinetics of the nanozyme toward TMB oxidation, different concentrations of TMB (0.01to1.0mM) were employed under typical reaction conditions (5m g iron oxide, 500mM H2O2in80m L of0.2M NaAc buffer, pH3.5, room temperature). Similar reactions were also conducted for H2O2by varying its concentration from0.01to1.0M. The apparent kinetic parameters for both TMB and H2O2 ( Km and Vmax) were estimated by considering the Michaelis-Menten equation28and using the Lineweaver-Burk equation. 29 # 2.4Investigation of the nanozyme activity through scavenging hydroxyl radicals ( /C15OH) The formation of /C15OH radicals by the iron oxide nanozyme through the cleavage of H2O2was monitored by scavenging /C15OH radicals using isopropyl alcohol (IPA), methyl alcohol (MA), and terephthalic acid (TA). In a typical procedure, different amounts of IPA and TA were added into the nanozyme-oxidized blue coloured TMB solution (5m g of iron oxide, 700m MTMBand500mM H2O2in0.2M NaAc, pH3.5buffer) and the decrease in the colour intensity was monitored by both naked-eye observation and absorbance spectra. The TA reacts with /C15OH radicals, and the formation of2-hydroxy-terephthalic acid ( i.e. , scavenging of /C15OH radicals) was monitored by the fluorescence spectra obtained for hydroxy-terephthalic acid. In our experiments, different amounts of the iron oxide nanozyme (both g -Fe2O3and a -Fe2O3) ranging from1to40m g were added into a mixture solution consisting of a0.2M NaAC (300m L) buffer solution, 5m L of H2O2solution and20m L of0.2M TA followed by12h incubation in the dark. The fluorescence spectra were then measured by using a plate reader (Infinite200PRO, Tecan Life Science) at an excitation wavelength of315nm. # 2.5Naked-eye observation and colourimetric estimation of glucose To assess the glucose sensing capabilities of both a -Fe2O3and g -Fe2O3, 30m L of glucose solutions with different concentrations ranging from1.0m M to1000m M were mixed with3.0m L of20mg mL /C0 1glucose oxidase (GO x ) solution in PBS (10mM, pH7.4) and incubated for30minutes at45 1C. After that, 17m L of the TMB solution (1mg mL /C0 1in DMSO), 5m L of the nanozyme (IO) (1mg mL /C0 1 ) and78m L of0.2M NaAc buffer solution (pH3.5) were added into the mixture and incubated for10minutes in the dark at room temperature. The glucose detection was observed by the naked eye as indicated by the colour change of the TMB solution from colourless to a blue colour and estimated by using colourimetry. # 3. Results and discussion # 3.1Structural characterization of the nanozymes The synthesis of the ordered mesoporous IO samples ( g -Fe2O3and a -Fe2O3) was achieved by using the nanocasting strategy with CMK-3as the hard-template (the detailed experimental procedure is given in the Experimental section). The high structural stability and inter-pore connectivity of the starting hard template are critical for ensuring the successful replication of the ordered mesoporosity of the CMK-3template in the final mesoporous metal oxide product. Therefore, we have synthesized an ordered mesoporous carbon, CMK-3, with thicker walls ( d = 10.8nm), as shown in Table1. Fig. 1a presents the wideangle XRD diffraction patterns of the obtained mesoporous a -Fe2O3and g -Fe2O3, which show the reflection characteristics of the trigonal ( R3c ) a -Fe2O3 (ICDD No. 33-0664) and cubic g -Fe2O3 ( P4132) (ICDD No. 39-1346) crystal systems, respectively. The line broadening of the reflections can be attributed to the nanocrystalline nature of the pore wall. The SEM and TEM images of both g -Fe2O3and a -Fe2O3are depicted in Fig. S1 (ESI † ). The XRD patterns were refined by the Rietveld method. Fig. S2 (ESI † ) depicts the Rietveld refinements of the XRD patterns of mesoporous a -Fe2O3and g -Fe2O3, respectively. This disorder may be attributed to the repeated synthetic steps, which would collapse the original mesostructure of CMK-3, and random growth and orientation of the IO crystals during the nanocasting process. Furthermore, we analyzed the carbon content. The a -Fe2O3and g -Fe2O3samples possess0.67% and1.71%, respectively. Fig. 1b depicts the N2adsorption-desorption isotherms of mesoporous a -Fe2O3and g -Fe2O3, showing type IV isotherms with small hysteresis loops, which are characteristic of mesoporous materials. This is further supported by the broad pore size distribution (Fig. 1b; inset), which clearly indicates the formation of disordered mesostructure. Magnetic NPs are highly useful for magnet-based preconcentration, isolation, separation, and purification of target analytes from complex biological systems. Because of their small size and volume, magnetic NPs are mostly superparamagnetic, i.e. without the presence of an external magnetic field, the NPs possess no net magnetic moment; whereas, in the presence of an external magnetic field, a magnetic dipole is instigated, and a net arrangement of magnetic moments occur. The magnetic moment of NPs returns to its native random orientation when the external magnetic field is removed, and in turn, they become nonmagnetic. This characteristic is a prerequisite for magnetic manipulation of the sample. Fig. 2a and b displays the magnetization isotherms of mesoporous g -Fe2O3and bulkg -Fe2O3 (prepared without the CMK-3template) measured at various temperatures. It is well established that their domain structures greatly influence the magnetic properties of IOs along with their size and shape. 30The reduction of the crystal size to nanodimensions can result in competition between thermal energy and magnetic anisotropy, which affects the magnetic behaviour of the materials. In particular, this feature is observed in the case of g -Fe2O3, which results in the transition from a ferromagnetic state to a superparamagnetic state by decreasing the crystal size. 31The magnetic saturation of mesoporous g -Fe2O3sample (Fig. 2a) is lower than that of bulkg -Fe2O3sample (Fig. 2b). In addition, the S-shaped hysteresis loops with low values of coercivity ( H C) and remanent magnetization ( M R) along with a high value of saturation magnetization ( M S) clearly indicate that the mesoporous g -Fe2O3exhibits a superparamagnetic behaviour at room temperature in contrast to the ferromagnetic bulk g -Fe2O3. This is because the magnetic moment of each nanocrystallite structure is too small to be affected by thermal excitation. The H C observed in ferromagnetic bulk g -Fe2O3is due to the weak interaction between neighbouring nanocrystallites. This is further evident from the ZFC curve (Fig. S3a, ESI † ), which shows a broad cusp at B85K, owing to the blocking transition temperature, which is a typical characteristic of superparamagnetic behaviour and is not evident in the M -T curves of bulk g -Fe2O3 (Fig. S3b, ESI † ). 32The temperature dependence of the M -H curves is indicated by the increase in the broadness of the hysteresis loop at low temperatures of20K and70K. In contrast to the bulk sample, the M -H curves of Fig. 2M -H curves of (a) mesoporous g -Fe2O3, (b) bulk g -Fe2O3, (c) mesoporous a -Fe2O3and (d) bulk a -Fe2O3. mesoporous a -Fe2O3do not show antiferromagnetic ordering even at low temperatures of20K and70K, which clearly indicates the suppression of Morin-transitions owing to its smaller crystallite size (Fig. 2c and d). 33 # 3.2Nanozyme activity Similar to HRP, IO NPs can catalyze the oxidation reaction of TMB in the presence of H2O2at pH ranging from3.0-6.5or in an acidic buffer. 34This catalytic process converts the colourless TMB into a blue-coloured TMB (a charged transfer diamine complex), which gives a maximum absorbance at652nm. Upon the addition of an acid (or stop solution), the blue-coloured solution changes to a yellow-coloured solution with a maximum absorbance at450nm. This nanozyme-based reaction has widely been used to develop a large number of biosensors for the detection of H2O2, glucose, cellular biomarkers, proteins, bacteria, and clinically-relevant biomolecules and biomarkers (exosomes, DNA, RNA, autoantibodies, etc. ). 3,4,8Herein, we have intricately studied the nanozyme activity of two different crystal phases of mesoporous IO (mesoporous g -Fe2O3and a -Fe2O3). To examine the nanozyme activity of both mesoporous IO samples, we have performed a set of experiments in the presence and absence (referred to as a control) of the mesoporous iron oxide samples (Fig. 3a). Both samples were used to catalyze the TMB substrate solution by incubating for10min in the dark at room temperature. In comparison with the two sets of controls (without H2O2and without IOs), both mesoporous IO samples generate a clear blue-coloured solution, while the substrate solutions for the two controls remain colourless. As seen in Fig. 3b, both crystal phases show much higher absorbance (at652nm) than the two control samples. Surprisingly, the mesoporous g -Fe2O3sample generates a considerably higher absorbance compared to the control sample by almost48-fold (0.059vs. 2.941) (Fig. 3b). The probable mechanism for the IO (ferric oxide)-induced peroxidase mimetic activity is the presence of a large number of ferric ions (Fe3+ ) on the surface of the mesoporous IO, which initiates the oxidation of TMB by generating hydroxyl free radicals ( /C15OH) from H2O2following the Fenton reaction, as shown in eqn (1)-(4). 3,35The Fenton reaction is considered to follow two cascade steps, where initially H2O2attaches to the exposed surface of the nanozyme and the /C15OH free radical is generated in the second step. The rate constant for eqn (1) (the attachment of H2O2on the ferric ion surface) is much lower than that of step (3) (generation of the /C15OHradical) (0.002L vs. 76L mol /C0 1s /C0 1 ), presenting eqn (1) as the rate-limiting step. 16Therefore, the nanozyme activity predominantly depends on the Fig. 3 (a) Schematic illustration of the nanozyme activity of the mesoporous IO for the oxidation of TMB in the presence of H2O2. (b) The peroxidase-like activities of the two mesoporous IO samples in comparison with the two control samples. Experiments were carried out by using5m g of mesoporous iron oxide in a reaction volume of0.2mL, in0.2M NaAc buffer, pH3.5at25 1C. The H2O2concentration was500mM, and the TMB concentration was700m Munless otherwise stated. The maximum point was set at100%. (c) Time-dependent peroxidase-like activity of mesoporous a -Fe2O3and g -Fe2O3. exposed area, i.e. , the adsorption sites present on the nanozyme surface. Notably, the high peroxidase mimetic activity of the as-prepared IO samples at room temperature may be attributed to their mesoporous structure. The mesopores can improve the catalytic activity by increasing the adsorption of substrates (both H2O2and TMB). Also, the position of ferric ions and the presence of holes in the crystal of the IOs play essential roles in enhancing their catalytic activity for the Fenton reaction. As seen in Fig. 3c, the rate of increase in absorbance is much higher for g -Fe2O3than that for a -Fe2O3. Astoundingly, the mesoporous g -Fe2O3sample exhibits around a seven-times higher response (2.941vs. 0.407) than that of mesoporous a -Fe2O3. This may be ascribed to the fact that the crystal structure of g -Fe2O3possesses cation vacancies (in g -Fe2O3, all the irons exist as trivalent and the presence of cation vacancies gives rise to charge neutrality) at the octahedral position. 36In brief, the crystal structure g -Fe2O3 is generally obtained by creating one-third vacancies out of the8a (tetrahedral sites; A-sites) and16d (octahedral sites; B-sites) Fe sites in the Fd3m space group. 37The crystallographic formula of g -Fe2O3can be represented as [Fe] 8a [Fe5/3 & 1/3 ] 16d O4where & is a symbol that represents the vacancies. Furthermore, Oosterhout and Rooijmans studies revealed that the spinel tetragonal superstructure of g -Fe2O3possessed an ordered distribution of the vacancies with tetragonal symmetry and a threetimes doubling along the c -axis, where the Fe atoms are completely ordered. 37,38These characteristic cation vacancies and the ordered exposure of Fe atoms present on the g -Fe2O3surface can facilitate increased adsorption of H2O2, triggering the ratelimiting step (eqn (1)). However, these cation vacancies are not present on the surface of a -Fe2O3, making it less attractive towards the substrate H2O2. This excellent phenomenon results in the relatively weak H2O2attachment as well as sevenfold lower activity of a -Fe2O3compared to that of g -Fe2O3. Moreover, we have also compared the nanozyme activity (towards TMB) of the mesoporous g -Fe2O3with the non-porous g -Fe2O3samples, and it has been found that the mesoporous IO gives almost fivetimes (% of activity; 100vs. 21.35) higher activity than that of the non-porous IO sample (Fig. S4, ESI † ), which is due to the pore-induced higher surface area and exposed Fe atoms. To check further, we have employed another chromogenic substrate 2,2 0 -azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulphonic acid) (ABTS) to assess the nanozyme activity of both crystal phases. Both nanozymes catalyze the oxidation of ABTS and generate a greencoloured solution with a characteristic absorbance at405nm. 39Similar to the case of TMB, the mesoporous g -Fe2O3sample exhibits around4times higher absorbance (0.4658vs. 1.5587at405nm) than the mesoporous a -Fe2O3sample (Fig. S5, ESI † ). To investigate the mechanism involved in the nanozyme activity toward the oxidation of TMB, hydroxyl radical scavenging experiments were performed. In our experiments, IPA and MA were added to the nanozyme-oxidized TMB solution, and the decrease of the colour intensity and absorbance were measured. IPA and MA have been considered as scavengers of hydroxyl radicals, and hence they reduce the number of hydroxyl radicals present in the system. 40As seen in Fig. S6 (ESI † ), IPA and MA reduce the colour intensity (fading of the blue colour compared to the control) and absorbance (at652nm) of both mesoporous g -Fe2O3and a -Fe2O3, demonstrating the TMB oxidation progresses through the formation of /C15OHradicals. With an increasing amount of IPA and MA, the peroxidase mimetic activity of mesoporous g -Fe2O3decreased, where an equivalent amount (% v/v) of IPA and MA decreases the activity to around78% and68% respectively, revealing that the scavengers absorb /C15OH radicals and hence lessen the absorbance (at652nm). To gain deeper insights into the participation of the hydroxyl radical, another set of experiments were conducted, in which TA was employed to react with the hydroxyl radical generated by the nanozyme activity of the iron oxides. TA reacts with /C15OH radicals and creates a highly fluorescent compound, 2-hydroxy terephthalic acid, which exhibits emission at430nm at an excitation wavelength of315nm. 41As seen in Fig. S7 (ESI † ), without the nanozyme, the control sample (0m g) does not generate any fluorescence signal, whereas the sample containing the nanozyme generates fluorescence signals. With increasing nanozyme amount (from1to40m g), the amount of generated /C15OH radicals increases, which results in the higher fluorescence signals of2-hydroxy terephthalic acid, representing the proportional relationship between the amount of nanozyme and the quantity of /C15OH radicals generated. These results evidently demonstrated and confirmed that both iron oxide nanozymes cleaved the H2O2to generate /C15OH radicals, which subsequently promotes the oxidation of TMB (or ABTS). It is noteworthy that the mesoporous g -Fe2O3gives a much more intense fluorescence spectrum (Fig. S8a, ESI † ) than the mesoporous a -Fe2O3 (Fig. S8b, ESI † ). Similar to the natural enzyme HRP, the nanozyme activity of IO is dependent on the substrate pH, and the amount of IO used. To study the effect of pH, we have studied the nanozyme activity of both mesoporous IO samples by using solutions with different pH ranging from2.5to5.5. With increasing pH value, the nanozyme activity of both mesoporous IO samples decreases (Fig. S8, ESI † ). This may be due to the excessive generation of FeOOH2+ and HO2 /C15 , which convert to molecular oxygen, thereby reducing the nanozyme activity. 3Although pH2.5gives the highest activity among all the pH values, under acidic conditions (pH less than3.0), leaching of iron from the mesoporous IO samples may occur. Therefore, pH3.5was chosen as the optimal pH for this study. The amount of nanozyme ( i.e. mesoporous IO) can also affect the activity. As shown in Fig. S9 (ESI † ), the nanozyme activity increases with an increasing amount of IO added into the reaction. As substrate solutions containing5, 10, 15and20m g of IO give nearly similar responses (absorbance), 5m g was selected as the optimum amount for subsequent experiments. To further explore the peroxidase mimetic activity of both mesoporous g -Fe2O3and a -Fe2O3, the apparent steady-state kinetics toward TMB and H2O2oxidation were ascertained by varying the concentrations of TMB and H2O2by using the initial rate method. 3Similar to HRP, a regular MichaelisMenten curve was obtained for both mesoporous g -Fe2O3and a -Fe2O3within the examined concentration ranges for both TMB (Fig. 4a and c) and H2O2 (Fig. 4b and d). The catalytic parameters Km (known as the Michaelis-Menten constant) and Vmax were evaluated from the typical Lineweaver-Burk doublereciprocal plot (1/[ V0] vs. 1/[ S ]) (Fig. 4, inset). 28,29The Km value signifies the affinity of the enzyme (nanozyme) toward the substrate. A lower Km value implies a higher affinity of the nanozyme for its substrate and vice versa . The estimated Km for mesoporous g -Fe2O3toward TMB is significantly lower compared to HRP (0.0997vs. 0.434mM), implying that the mesoporous g -Fe2O3sample exhibits a much higher nanozyme activity for TMB than natural HRP. This outcome indicates the potential of the as-prepared mesoporous g -Fe2O3as a substitute for HRP. However, in the case of mesoporous a -Fe2O3, the Km value is almost similar or slightly higher (0.5304vs. 0.434mM) than that of HRP, indicating that the mesoporous a -Fe2O3sample is not a suitable alternative to HRP. Nevertheless, the Km values of both the mesoporous a -Fe2O3and g -Fe2O3samples toward H2O2are substantially higher than HRP (144.30and127.92, respectively, vs. 3.70mM), implying that a higher amount of H2O2is needed to achieve significant nanozyme activity (at room temperature) for the two mesoporous IO samples. One of the possible reasons for the enhanced nanozyme activity of both mesoporous IO samples (at room temperature) is their mesopore-induced high surface area (enabling more Fe3+ ions to interact with the substrate) and interaction volume. These factors augment the mass transfer and cascade catalysis by keeping the substrates on the surface moiety and increasing the rate of electron transfer, thereby proliferating the kinetics of the oxidation reaction. Another probable reason is the transfer of a higher amount of lone-pair electron cloud (charge) from the amino group (TMB) to the vacant d-orbital of Fe3+ , which enhances the mobility and electron density of the IOs to enhance the catalytic activity. 3,42Nonetheless, the significantly lower Km value of g -Fe2O3than that of a -Fe2O3indicates its superiority for biosensing. As stated earlier, a -Fe2O3possesses a rhombohedral-centred hexagonal structure of the corundum type with a close-packed oxygen lattice with the R3ch (167) space group, where two-thirds of the octahedral sites are occupied by Fe3+ ions. 43On the other hand, g -Fe2O3possesses a cubic crystal structure of an inverse spinel type and crystallised in the Fd3m space group, where the oxygen anions have a cubic close-packed array and the Fe3+ ions spread out throughout the tetrahedral sites (FeA) and the octahedral sites (FeB) (inset; Fig. 3). 36 Fig. 4Steady-state kinetics and catalytic mechanism of mesoporous Fe2O3. Moreover, the crystal structure of g -Fe2O3possesses cation vacancies in the octahedral sites. Besides, most of these cation vacancies are located on the surface of g -Fe2O3. These sites act as adsorption sites for the substrates. Therefore, the mesoporous g -Fe2O3can adsorb and hold a much higher amount of H2O2as well as TMB, leading to its significantly higher peroxidase-mimetic activity toward TMB oxidation. As evident from Table S1 (ESI † ), the mesoporous g -Fe2O3sample shows a much better nanozyme performance compared to previously reported iron oxide-based nanomaterials and composites. For instance, recently reported mesoporous Fe2O3nanoflakes exhibited a Km value of0.24mM at room temperature, which is almost three times higher than this work. 4In another research study, Prussian blue-modified g -Fe2O3exhibited a Km value of0.307mM for TMB oxidation at room temperature, which is even higher than the Km value of the as-prepared mesoporous g -Fe2O3. 13Fe3O4NPs were reported to possess a very similar Km value to this work (0.098vs. 0.0997mM); however, this activity was achieved at40 1C. 34This high temperature is not suitable for sensing of biological molecules, such as proteins, antibodies or cells, as biomolecules tend to degrade at elevated temperatures. 44In the past few years, several composite materials and biological molecule-based nanozymes with low Km values toward TMB oxidation have been reported. For instance, ATPmediated Fe3O4showed a three-times higher Km value than this report. However, this was achieved by modifying the Fe3O4NPs with adenosine triphosphate (ATP) and carrying out the nanozymecatalytic reaction at40 1C. 12Recently, adenosine5-monophosphate (AMP)-modified Fe3O4was also reported, where the modification of the Fe3O4NPs with phosphate-containing adenosine analogs greatly enhanced the peroxidase mimetic activity toward the oxidation of amplex ultra-red (AU) (a Km value of0.036mM) and the adenosine analogs followed the trend: AMP-Fe3O4 4ATP-Fe3O4. 14To achieve such high activity, the Fe3O4NPs needed to be modified with AMP under proper conditions, including a high temperature of37 1C. Remarkably, our mesoporous g -Fe2O3can achieve a similarly high level of activity without any chemical or biological modification, indicating the benefit of the mesoporous structure and importance of phase tuning. # 3.3Detection and estimation of glucose In order to check the applicability and superiority of the mesoporous g -Fe2O3over a -Fe2O3, we have utilized both nanozymes for colourimetric (naked eye) glucose detection. Glucose is one of the most prominent biomolecules, and its aberrancy signifies the onset and progression of several diseases, especially diabetes mellitus and diabetes-related diseases, such as kidney failure, heart disease, and blindness. 45,46Therefore, a great deal of research has been carried out to develop robust and simple point-of-care (POC) diagnostics to monitor and manage glucoseassociated diseases in an easy and simple manner. In recent years, nanozymes have shown promising potential in glucose sensors. 47 In a typical nanozyme-based glucose detection, glucose solution was initially oxidized by the GO x enzyme to generate glucose equivalent to the generated H2O2, which is subsequently detected by employing a TMB substrate solution and nanozyme, as depicted in Fig. 5a. 48In the presence of the generated H2O2, the nanozyme oxidizes the colourless TMB solution to a blue colour TMBox charge-transfer complex solution, which can be quantified by measuring its absorbance at652nm. The intensity or absorbance of the generated blue colour is equivalent to the generated H2O2as well as the amount of glucose present in the sample. To check the capability of both mesoporous IO samples toward glucose detection, we have employed both samples as nanozymes with the same concentration of glucose solution (250m M). In comparison to the control sample (no mesoporous IO), the mesoporous a -Fe2O3sample generates a pale blue colour, whereas the mesoporous g -Fe2O3sample generates a highly intense blue-coloured solution (inset, Fig. 5b). The mesoporous g -Fe2O3sample gives a much higher absorbance of0.816 (at652nm) compared to0.089for mesoporous a -Fe2O3, indicating its superiority and excellent potentiality for glucose detection. To estimate the sensitivity for glucose detection, a series of diluted glucose solutions with concentrations ranging from1m Mto1mM were tested. As shown in Fig. 5d (inset), there is an increasing trend in the intensity of the blue colour and the absorbance with an increasing concentration of glucose (Fig. 5c). (b) The mean responses of absorbance obtained for the assay with mesoporous a -Fe2O3, mesoporous g -Fe2O3, and the control sample (NoT, PBS was used instead of glucose). The inset shows the corresponding color change. (c) The absorbance spectra obtained for varying concentrations of standard glucose samples ranging from1m Mto1000m M. The corresponding bar diagram for standard glucose concentrations is depicted in (d) (the inset shows the corresponding colour change). (e) Calibration curve for glucose detection using g -Fe2O3as a nanozyme with varying glucose concentrations from1m M to1000m M. by GO x , which consequently enhances the nanozyme-catalyzed oxidation of TMB. As evident in Fig. 5(e), there is a linear increase in absorbance (colourimetric readout) with the increased amount of glucose. The linear regression equation can be approximated as y = 0.2133x /C0 0.1429, with a r2 (correlation coefficient) of0.9603. The limit of detection (LOD) for the naked eye was evaluated by observing the colour intensity and found to be1m M, which is clearly distinguishable from the no-target/ template (NoT) control (PBS was used instead of glucose). The colourimetric LOD was also evaluated by considering a signal-tonoise ratio of3.0and determined to be0.9m M with good interassay reproducibility (relative standard deviation o5%, for n = 3), which is much more sensitive than recently reported nanozymebased glucose sensors. For instance, this assay is almost ten-times more sensitive (a LOD of0.9m M vs. 8.16m M) than recently reported GO x @ZIF-8(NiPd) nanoflowers. 49A very similar LOD (0.9m M vs. 1.12m M) for glucose was reported for Fe3O4@C yolk-shell nanoparticles. However, such a low LOD was achieved by conducting the TMBoxidation reaction at60 1C. 50Such a high-temperature reaction is not suitable for achieving a simple and portable glucose sensor. The mesoporous g -Fe2O3sample has shown good potential to replace natural enzymes for TMB oxidation and reduce the burdens (cost, handling, and storage) associated with natural enzymes. Moreover, it exhibits good stability even after seven days of preparation. The application of this mesoporous IO sample is not limited to glucose detection; it may also be applied as an ideal alternative to conventional ELISA-based sensors for the detection of many other clinically relevant protein-based biomarkers by choosing proper recognition molecules, targets, and antibody functionalization with the signalling nanozyme. Notably, the as-prepared mesoporous IO samples (without any modification and functionalization) are capable of oxidizing TMB even at room temperature, which renders them highly suitable for paper and strip-based nanozymemodified biosensors. # 4. Conclusions This work has demonstrated that mesoporous IO samples with two different crystal phases ( a - and g -) exhibit different peroxidase mimetic activity. Both mesoporous a -Fe2O3and g -Fe2O3exhibit room-temperature peroxidase mimetic activity. Owing to the favourable crystal phase, the mesoporous g -Fe2O3sample exhibited around seven-times higher responses in peroxidase activity than the mesoporous a -Fe2O3sample. The applicability of these mesoporous IOs as nanozymes was verified by the colourimetric detection of glucose, where the mesoporous g -Fe2O3sample shows almost an eight times higher response than mesoporous a -Fe2O3. The mesoporous g -Fe2O3sample exhibits very high sensitivity for colourimetric glucose detection with an impressive LOD of0.9m M. The findings of this work will provide an in-depth understanding of the effect of the crystal phase of IOs on their peroxidase mimetic activity. We envisage that this work will provide useful guidance for designing highly effective nanozymes based on IO nanoarchitectures for the sensing of glucose and other biomolecules in the future. # Conflicts of interest There are no conflicts to declare. # Acknowledgements This work was supported by the Australian Research Council (ARC) Discovery Project (DP190102944). All the samples were systematically characterized by various analytical, spectroscopic and imaging techniques. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained by using a Bruker D8Focus Advance Diffractometer equipped with Lynx Eye detector with a Ni-filtered Cu K α radiation ( λ = 1.5406 Å) operating at40kV and40mA in2  range of1.0to7.0with a step size of0.01  and a step time of2s. signature.instructions:
You are helpful assistant in chemistry, specializing in nanozymes. Your task is to analyze scientific articles and extract detailed information about various experiments with nanozymes. It is crucial for you to accurately and comprehensively describe each experiment separately, without referring to other experiments in the article.

Usually, the articles contain several experiments with nanozymes with different parameters, such as:
- Formula (e.g. Fe3O4)
- Activity (usually peroxidase, oxidase, catalase or laccase)
- Syngony (usually cubic, hexagonal, tetragonal, monoclinic, orthorhombic, trigonal, amorphous or triclinic)
- Length, width and depth (or just size or diameter)
- Surface chemistry (naked by default or poly(ethylene oxide), poly(N-Vinylpyrrolidone), Tetrakis(4-carboxyphenyl)porphine or other)
- Polymer used in synthesis (none or poly(N-Vinylpyrrolidone), oleic acid, poly(ethylene oxide), BSA or other)
- Surfactant (none or l-ascorbic acid, ethylene glycol, sodium citrate, cetrimonium bromide, citric acid, trisodium citrate, ascorbic acid or other)
- Molar mass, Michaelis constant Km, molar maximum reaction rate Vmax
- Reaction type (substrat + co-substrat) (TMB + H2O2, H2O2 + TMB, TMB, ABTS + H2O2, H2O2, OPD + H2O2, H2O2 + GSH or other)
- Minimum concentration of the substrate when measuring catalytic activity C_min (mM)
- Maximum concentration of the substrate when measuring catalytic activity C_max (mM)
- Concentration of the co-substrate when measuring the catalytic activity (mM)
- Concentration of nanoparticles in the measurement of catalytic (mg/mL)
- pH at which the catalytic activity was measured and temperature at which the research was carried out (°C).

You need to find all the experiments with different values mentioned in the article and extraction them as separate objects. It's imperative that each of these elements is addressed independently for every experiment, providing a complete and isolated description with accurate measurements in appropriate units. This approach will ensure a comprehensive and clear understanding of each experiment as an individual entity within the scientific literature on nanozymes.

CRITICAL RULES FOR EXTRACTION:
1. Keep the numerical values in the right units of measurement. It is critically important to extract all the numerical values as in the example, especially important are formula, activity, syngony, length, width, depth (size or diameter), Km, Vmax, reaction type.
2. Usually such parameters as Michaelis constant Km (mM), Vmax, mM/s are obtained in two experiments for every type of nanoparticle. You must determine what type of reaction such parameters as Michaelis constant Km (mM), Vmax, mM/s belong to.

LOGIC FOR REACTION TYPES (TRACKS):
- Reaction type is H2O2+TMB when H2O2 is a substrate and TMB in co-cubstrate.
- Reaction type is TMB +H2O2 when TMB is a substrate and H2O2 in co-cubstrate.

For example in pair H2O2 and TMB:
- In first case (you call this case as Reaction type TMB+H2O2): H2O2 plays role as a co-substrate with constant concentration(C(const), mM) and TMB plays role as a substrate with concentrations from Cmin,mM to Cmax, mM.
- In second case (you call this case as Reaction type H2O2+TMB): TMB plays role as a co-substrate with constant concentration(C(const), mM) and H2O2 plays role as a substrate with concentrations from Cmin,mM to Cmax, mM.

Please divide all the data into 2 tracks: where H2O2 was a substrate and its concentration varied and where H2O2 was a co-substrate and had a constant concentration. 
Please show data only for those nanoparticles for which the kinetic assay was performed. All other parameters from the example are also important.
Do not attempt to use knowledge you already have.
